topic: Schools should serve only vegetarian meals at least once a week

systemArguments.text: Mandatory vegetarian meals could lower participation rates in school meals, hurting concentration and learning
Vegetarian meals are often more expensive than traditional meals, straining already tight school budgets
Growing children need complete proteins from meat for proper physical and cognitive development
Parents should have the right to decide what their children eat, not school administrators
School cafeteria staff lack training and experience in preparing appealing vegetarian meals
Mandatory vegetarian days impose specific dietary beliefs on families from diverse cultural backgrounds
Students with food allergies or sensitivities may have fewer safe options on vegetarian-only days
Local farmers and meat suppliers will lose important contracts, hurting the regional economy

goldenArguments.text: Parents have the fundamental right to control their children's dietary choices
Cultural and religious freedom includes the right to maintain traditional eating practices
Government institutions should not impose ideological dietary preferences on citizens
Mandatory vegetarian meals could reduce vital nutrition like protein and iron
Many students will refuse to eat vegetarian meals and go hungry, affecting their concentration and academic performance
Vegetarian-only policies increase food costs and administrative burden for schools
Limited meal options create safety risks for students with dietary restrictions
Forced dietary changes harm local agricultural communities and food suppliers

humanRationale: System arguments effectively capture most key opposition points with good detail and practical concerns. They cover parental rights (arg4), cultural issues (arg6), nutrition concerns (arg1, arg3), cost factors (arg2), implementation challenges (arg5), and economic impact (arg8). The golden arguments separate value-based objections (parental rights, cultural freedom, government overreach) from policy-focused concerns (nutrition, costs, safety, economic impact). System shows strong alignment with both categories but could be more concise in separating values from practical outcomes.
